ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken foot food product class (FOODON_03302051). No specific mala-spiced chicken feet entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers chicken feet as a raw/generic category. The mala spice preparation is not separately coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for mala-spiced chicken feet or chicken feet as a prepared food product.

main_ingredients: mala spice blend (Sichuan peppercorn, dried chilli, star anise, cinnamon, fennel, clove)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chicken feet mala is approximately 150 g, comprising 4–6 chicken feet dressed in mala spice sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken feet, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05358) adapted with mala seasoning adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken feet (FDC ID 171477, 'Chicken, feet, cooked, NS as to cooking method'). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect mala sauce seasoning (soy sauce, salt) typical of restaurant preparation. Sugar and carbohydrate adjusted to reflect added sugar and starch in mala marinade. Values represent a best-estimate composite for mala-dressed chicken feet as served in a Burmese-Chinese restaurant context. Confidence: medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-based dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked chicken and mixed spiced meat preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mala chicken feet. GI estimated at 32 based on analogy with high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked poultry preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database. Chicken and similar protein-dominant meat dishes consistently show GI in the 25–40 range due to negligible starch content. Mala sauce adds a small amount of sugar and starch (estimated carbohydrate_g ~3.8 per 100g), which does not materially elevate GI. GL per 150g serving = (32 × 3.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.8, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from mala sauce and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid mala chicken feet due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (780 mg per 150 g serving), which represents over one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mala chicken feet have a low estimated glycemic index (~32) and low carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g), resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per serving, making this dish generally suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, the high sodium content warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium; collagen-rich protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mala chicken feet contain moderate saturated fat (~3.6 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~520 mg per 100 g), both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions, and should be aware that the mala spice blend may also contain pro-inflammatory compounds at high intake levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Feet Mala (ကြက်ခြေထောက်မာလာထောင်း) is a Burmese-Chinese fusion dish featuring chicken feet dressed in a mala (麻辣) spice blend, combining the numbing heat of Sichuan peppercorn with the fiery intensity of dried chillies, enriched with soy sauce, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and a touch of sugar and vinegar. The dish is typically prepared by first boiling or pressure-cooking the chicken feet until the skin and tendons are tender and gelatinous, then marinating or tossing them in the mala sauce, often served cold or at room temperature as a snack or appetiser. The texture is characteristically gelatinous and chewy due to the high collagen content of chicken feet, with a bold, complex flavour profile that is simultaneously spicy, numbing, savoury, and slightly sweet. Nutritionally, chicken feet are rich in collagen and connective tissue protein (~19 g protein per 100 g), moderate in fat (~13.5 g per 100 g), and low in carbohydrates (~3.8 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 215 kcal per 100 g. The mala seasoning significantly elevates sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g), making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (~32), and the glycemic load per 150 g serving is approximately 1.8, making it suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions. This dish is popular in Myanmar and among Burmese communities in Singapore, often available at Burmese restaurants and hawker stalls. The mala flavour profile reflects the influence of Yunnan-Chinese culinary traditions on Burmese cuisine.

confidence_reason: No exact match for mala-spiced chicken feet was found in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken feet (FDC ID 171477) with seasoning adjustments for mala sauce. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate poultry preparations. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken feet (FDC ID 171477) with upward sodium adjustment to reflect mala sauce seasoning; this is an estimation and actual restaurant values may vary. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for cooked chicken feet. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for cooked chicken feet. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~32, low category) based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked poultry preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mala chicken feet exists in the literature. 5. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain rice wine or non-halal soy sauce depending on restaurant preparation; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON listed 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee' which appear to be erroneous tags from the field survey platform and have been disregarded. 7. The food is served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant in Singapore, reflecting the Burmese diaspora culinary context. 8. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given that this dish is served in Singapore, with additional Burmese romanized form included for cultural completeness.
